metadata.summary: Navigation hub for the three-phase SDD lifecycle: Spec Phase (requirements) → Design Phase (technical blueprint) → Execution Phase (implementation). Provides phase-by-phase input/output tables, complete workflow path, and links to detailed phase files.

content.title: SDD Workflows Navigation Hub
content.summary: Navigation hub for the three-phase SDD lifecycle: Spec Phase (requirements) → Design Phase (technical blueprint) → Execution Phase (implementation). Provides phase-by-phase input/output tables, a complete workflow path, and links to the detailed phase files. This file routes; it does not instruct.

why.p: Agents receiving a vague task like "work on feature X" need to locate the current lifecycle state before doing anything. Without a navigation entry point they either re-derive the phase from artifact existence checks or jump straight to execution on an unapproved spec, producing work that must be discarded. This hub makes the three-phase structure and transition criteria explicit in one place.

list.item: Starting work on a feature without knowing which SDD phase is active
Bootstrapping a new project: constitution through first execution
Switching phases mid-feature (spec approved, starting design)
Explaining the SDD system to a new agent or contributor
Already in a specific phase and know what to do — go directly to the phase file
As a substitute for phase-specific workflow files: this file is navigation, not instructions
Looking for document templates — those live in template-spec, template-design, template-task

section.name: three-phase-structure
section.p: SPEC PHASE → DESIGN PHASE → EXECUTION PHASE

list.item: Spec Phase: requirements gathering, problem definition, constitution, roadmap. Output: approved spec.md
Design Phase: technical blueprint, task breakdown, parallelization. Output: approved design.md + implementation-plan.md + TASK_*.md
Execution Phase: task execution, quality control, reflexion learning. Output: committed, tested code
Constitution: input=project vision or codebase → output=constitution.md
Backlog Grooming: input=backlog features → output=refined specs, designs
Roadmapping: input=completed features + new ideas → output=roadmap.md
Parallelization: input=all TASK_*.md → output=execution plan with waves
Task Execution: input=TASK_*.md + SDD context → output=code, tests, commits
Quality Gates: input=code + SDD docs → output=pass/fail with feedback
Reflexion: input=task results → output=updated patterns.md / mistakes.md

rule.statement: Do not advance to the next phase until all outputs from the current phase exist and have the correct status (Draft is not Approved).
rule.rationale: Phase confusion is the most common agent error. Starting design on a Draft spec produces architectural decisions based on unstable requirements. The phase transition is gated by human approval of the phase output.

rule.statement: Human approval is required at every phase boundary: spec Draft → Approved, design Draft → Approved, implementation-plan Draft → Approved.
rule.rationale: Agents self-approve their own outputs when not constrained. The status field change from Review to Approved must be a human action; agents set Draft → Review only.

list.item: 70-89%: Clarify gaps, present alternatives before proceeding
< 70%: Stop, ask questions first — do not proceed

rule.statement: Apply confidence thresholds at every phase transition, not only at execution time.
rule.rationale: Agents apply the 90% threshold only at execution because that is where it is most visible. Spec → Design and Design → Execution transitions have equal risk of proceeding with insufficient confidence.

rule.statement: Quality gate levels L1-L6 apply to the execution phase only; do not apply them to spec or design documents.
rule.rationale: Agents sometimes refuse to write a thorough design doc "because it might be too long" (misapplying L2/L3 thinking). Quality gates are for code artifacts, not planning documents.

list.item: Step 5: Task Creation (design-phase) → generate TASK_*.md files

rule.statement: Each task must fit within 100k token context window: research ~20k + task file ~5k + implementation ~50k + buffer ~25k = total < 100k.
rule.rationale: Claude's effective context for high-quality code generation degrades above ~80k tokens. The 100k rule with a 25k buffer ensures the executor can read all context, produce the implementation, and run tests without hitting coherence limits.

rule.statement: The 100k rule applies to execution-phase tasks only — not to spec or design documents.
rule.rationale: Agents misapply the rule to design docs, producing truncated architectural decisions because "the document would be too long." Design docs should be as long as they need to be; only tasks are token-bounded.

rule.statement: Group tasks with no unmet dependencies into the same wave; execute tasks within a wave in parallel; checkpoint between waves.
rule.rationale: Wave execution is the mechanism for parallelization. Without it, all tasks run sequentially even when independent. Typical speedup: 1.8x-3.5x. Checkpoints between waves catch integration failures before they cascade.

list.item: Wave 1: independent tasks — run in parallel
Checkpoint 1: verify all Wave 1 outputs, merge
Wave 2: tasks depending on Wave 1 — run in parallel
